name: Octagon Verdict Data
defaultScope: Judge UFC accomplishments by default. Only mention scope when it matters.

guidance.sourceOfTruth: Use this Action feed over uploaded Knowledge, memory, web browsing, or old scores.
avoid: Raw category point totals in normal answers

fighters.name: Jon Jones
fighters.oneLiner: The benchmark UFC resume: unmatched title-fight success, elite quality wins, long-term dominance, and no true competitive loss.
fighters.titleSummary: Normal 1.00. Interim 0.75. Vacant undisputed 0.90. Second division undisputed 1.25. Vacant second division 1.…
fighters.bestArgument: Jones ranks #1 because he has the strongest UFC championship resume ever, the best title-fight win total, elite wins across multiple eras,…

fighters.name: Georges St-Pierre
fighters.oneLiner: The complete UFC resume: a legendary welterweight reign, elite quality wins, and one of the cleanest prime runs in the sport.
fighters.primeRecord: 18-1 after first Hughes loss; Serra is the counted prime finish loss
fighters.titleSummary: Normal 1.00. Interim Hughes 3 = 0.75. Bisping second-division undisputed title = 1.25. Total title fight wins…
fighters.bestArgument: St-Pierre ranks #2 because he combines an all-time welterweight title reign with the strongest quality-wins case in the UFC, elite consiste…
fighters.counter: Jon Jones still has the edge in championship volume and total time at the very top. St-Pierre's case is elite across the board, but the Ser…

fighters.name: Demetrious Johnson
fighters.oneLiner: The defining UFC flyweight champion: historic title control, elite technical dominance, and one of the cleanest prime skill sets in the spo…
fighters.titleSummary: DJ’s title case is historic at flyweight: long reign, repeated defenses, and clean separation from the divisi…
fighters.primeSummary: His UFC prime was long, technical, consistent, and rarely chaotic; he controlled fights without many ugly dips.
fighters.bestArgument: DJ’s best argument is technical completeness plus title control: he was cleaner than Anderson, more dominant than most longer-resume fighte…
fighters.counter: DJ’s best counterargument is skill and cleanliness. He may not have the biggest names, but his technical control and consistency are almost…
fighters.edge: DJ wins when the debate rewards complete dominance, clean title control, and fewer resume holes over bigger-name but messier cases.
bestWins: Benavidez, Dodson, Cejudo, Horiguchi, and years of flyweight title defenses give DJ a clean dominance case even after division-strength context.
notes: Flyweight dominance is fully respected, but opponent-quality value is division-adjusted.
Cruz at bantamweight and Cejudo in a close flyweight title fight matter, but neither collapses the prime case.
DJ’s resume is built on championship consistency. He ruled flyweight for years, defended repeatedly, and rarely looked…
His championship case is historically strong, even with division-strength context. The title reign was long, stable, an…

fighters.name: Anderson Silva
fighters.oneLiner: The peak-aura case: historic middleweight title control, terrifying finishing dominance, and one of the most iconic prime runs in UFC histo…
fighters.titleSummary: Anderson’s title case is a long middleweight reign built on repeated defenses, spectacular finishes, and one…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime aura lasted for years, but the Weidman losses create drag because they land before the model fully shifts him…
fighters.bestArgument: Anderson’s best argument is peak aura: few fighters ever felt more dangerous, more creative, or more inevitable during a title reign.
fighters.counter: Anderson’s argument is aura and peak fear factor. If someone values the most terrifying prime, he has one of the strongest cases ever.
fighters.edge: Anderson wins comparisons when peak impact, finishing danger, and iconic championship dominance outweigh cleaner but less explosive resumes.
bestWins: Franklin, Henderson, Griffin, Belfort, Sonnen, Okami, and years of middleweight title wins give Anderson one of the sport’s most iconic reigns.
notes: Middleweight dominance is central to the case, but opponent-quality value is slightly division-adjusted.
Weidman losses count as in-prime drag; later losses are mostly post-prime context.
Anderson’s resume is built on title reign plus aura. He ruled middleweight for years, finished challengers spectacularl…
His championship case is huge: a long middleweight reign, repeated title wins, and a stretch where he felt like the mos…

fighters.name: Islam Makhachev
fighters.oneLiner: The modern lightweight control case: elite finishing efficiency, high-end prime dominance, and a title run that keeps getting stronger.
fighters.titleSummary: Islam’s title case has grown into a deeper modern lightweight reign, with more championship volume than a sho…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime began later than Khabib’s scoring window but has already stacked elite years against modern lightweight and p…
fighters.bestArgument: Islam’s best argument is modern depth: elite skill, growing title volume, quality wins across styles, and a prime run that is still strengt…
fighters.counter: The counterargument against Islam is that he still does not feel as untouchable as Khabib. The resume may be bigger, but the aura is not qu…
fighters.edge: Islam wins when the debate rewards total resume over pure peak. His championship volume and elite-win depth have become too much to ignore.
bestWins: Oliveira, Volkanovski, Poirier, Tsarukyan, and Hooker give Islam a deep modern lightweight ledger with real championship weight.
notes: Modern lightweight strength is a positive, and the later welterweight piece adds extra title-level value.
The Martins loss is pre-prime and counts lightly; his prime scoring window starts around Drew Dober.
Islam’s resume has grown into one of the strongest lightweight cases. The championship volume and elite-win depth have…
His championship case is now the separator from Khabib. He has more title-fight volume and more time proving the belt a…

fighters.name: Khabib Nurmagomedov
fighters.oneLiner: The cleanest prime run at lightweight: unbeaten in the UFC, overwhelming round control, and the strongest dominance case in this ranking.
fighters.titleSummary: Khabib’s title resume is compact, but his defenses came against elite lightweights and he retired unbeaten on…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime run from RDA through Gaethje was one of the cleanest dominance stretches ever: short compared with long-reign…
fighters.bestArgument: Khabib’s best argument is purity: no UFC losses, no real collapse, and a final stretch that looked like total separation from elite lightwe…
fighters.counter: Khabib’s argument against almost anyone is purity. No losses, no real collapse, no post-prime damage, and one of the clearest primes ever.
fighters.edge: Khabib wins when the debate rewards dominance, perfection, and how unbeatable a fighter looked at his best.
bestWins: RDA, Barboza, McGregor, Poirier, and Gaethje give Khabib a compact but extremely high-quality lightweight resume.
notes: Lightweight strength is a positive; his best work came in one of the toughest divisions.
No UFC losses and no prime finish losses.
Khabib’s resume is short compared with the biggest title-volume cases, but it is incredibly clean: no losses, elite lig…
The championship case is strong but compact. He beat elite lightweights and retired on top, but he does not have the lo…

fighters.name: Alexander Volkanovski
fighters.oneLiner: The complete featherweight champion case: title consistency, strong quality wins, and one of the deepest modern resumes in the sport.
fighters.titleSummary: Volk’s title case is a clean modern featherweight reign built around Holloway trilogy separation and repeated…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime was not as long as Holloway’s total run, but at his best he was the defining featherweight of the era.
fighters.bestArgument: Volk’s best argument is balance plus direct separation: title success, elite wins, Max trilogy control, and modern featherweight strength.
fighters.counter: Volk’s best argument is direct separation. He did not just share an era with Max; he beat him three times and became the defining featherwe…
fighters.edge: Volk wins when head-to-head separation, championship control, and modern featherweight strength matter more than raw career volume.
bestWins: Aldo, Holloway three times, Ortega, Korean Zombie, and Yair give Volk one of the cleanest modern featherweight title resumes.
notes: Modern featherweight strength helps his opponent-quality case.
Islam losses receive reduced up-division elite-loss treatment; Topuria is the main prime-dominance drag.
Volk’s resume is built on featherweight separation. He beat Aldo, took the belt from Holloway, won the trilogy, and def…
His championship case is extremely clean at featherweight. The Max trilogy gives him direct separation over another all…

fighters.name: Randy Couture
fighters.oneLiner: The old-school championship chaos case: heavyweight gold, light heavyweight gold, huge title moments, and a messy record built from constan…
fighters.titleSummary: Couture’s title case is built on winning UFC gold at heavyweight and light heavyweight, with repeated champio…
fighters.primeSummary: His best years were not one smooth prime; they were multiple veteran surges across divisions, which makes the case impr…
fighters.bestArgument: Couture ranks #8 because his UFC-only case is loaded with championship moments across heavyweight and light heavyweight. The record is mess…
fighters.counter: The case against Couture is the record. He has more losses than the cleaner GOAT candidates, so his ranking depends on how much credit you…
fighters.edge: Couture wins comparisons when championship moments, two-division relevance, and title-fight difficulty matter more than clean record aesthe…
bestWins: Sylvia, Liddell, Ortiz, Belfort, Rizzo twice, Randleman, Gonzaga, and Coleman give Couture one of the most important old-school UFC win lists.
notes: Heavyweight and light heavyweight era context matter because his value comes from repeated title-level risk.
Losses are real drag, but many came in title or high-risk fights across divisions.
Couture’s UFC case is built around title moments across two divisions. The losses hurt, but the championship achievemen…
The title case is the whole argument: multiple UFC championship runs, heavyweight and light heavyweight value, and majo…

fighters.name: Max Holloway
fighters.oneLiner: The volume case: relentless pace, elite quality wins, and one of the longest useful elite windows in the featherweight era.
fighters.titleSummary: Holloway’s title case is strong, but his all-time case is even more about volume, elite wins, and relevance b…
fighters.primeSummary: Max’s prime and relevance window is unusually long: he stayed elite through multiple phases, even after losing the feat…
fighters.bestArgument: Holloway ranks #9 because his quality-wins score and longevity score are both elite. Few fighters in this ranking have stacked as many mean…
fighters.counter: Max’s argument is total body of work. Even when someone peaked higher, Max often has more elite volume and more proof over time.
fighters.edge: Holloway wins when the debate rewards longevity, opponent volume, durability, and sustained elite relevance.
bestWins: Aldo twice, Ortega, Kattar, Yair, Korean Zombie, and Gaethje give Holloway one of the strongest depth arguments in the ranking.
notes: Featherweight value is strong, and the lightweight moments add context without becoming the core case.
Loss volume matters, but several losses came against elite opponents or across a long elite window.
Holloway’s resume is built on volume and staying power. He kept beating top-tier names before, during, and after his ti…
His championship case is strong, but not as clean as Volk’s because the trilogy directly separates them in the same era.

fighters.name: Kamaru Usman
fighters.oneLiner: The post-GSP welterweight champion case: dominant title control, elite round winning, and a focused but powerful championship peak.
fighters.titleSummary: Usman’s title case is a strong modern welterweight reign with repeated defenses and real champion authority,…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime was excellent but tighter than GSP’s or Holloway’s: a dominant title window rather than a decade-long all-tim…
fighters.bestArgument: Usman ranks #10 because his welterweight title run had real champion authority. He paired elite round control with strong defenses and qual…
fighters.counter: Usman’s argument is champion peak. If the debate is who looked more in control at the top, Usman has a real lane.
fighters.edge: Usman wins when championship peak, round control, and title-defense authority outweigh longer but less dominant resumes.
bestWins: Woodley, Covington twice, Burns, Masvidal, Edwards, and RDA give Usman a strong modern welterweight ledger.
notes: Modern welterweight gives him strong quality-win value, though not quite GSP-level depth.
The Edwards finish and rematch loss hurt the back end, but they came against elite opposition.
Usman’s resume is built on championship authority. He ruled welterweight, defended repeatedly, and beat top contenders…
His championship case is stronger than his overall volume case. The title run was clean, controlled, and had real champ…

fighters.name: Jose Aldo
fighters.oneLiner: A UFC-only legend with real title value and longevity, but not a clean top-10 case once WEC is excluded and the McGregor/Holloway/Volk dama…
fighters.titleSummary: Aldo’s UFC title case is strong but incomplete without historical context because part of his legendary reign…
fighters.primeSummary: Aldo’s broader prime was long and legendary, while the UFC-only scored version is damaged by three finished losses and…
fighters.bestArgument: Aldo ranks in the top-15 range because the UFC-only model still respects his title work, quality wins, and ability to stay relevant across…
fighters.counter: Aldo’s argument is historical greatness plus longevity. If WEC is included, his case jumps. In this UFC-only ranking, that context is respe…
fighters.edge: Aldo wins debates when the opponent has weaker championship proof or less sustained elite relevance. He loses more often when the opponent…
bestWins: Edgar, Mendes, Korean Zombie, Lamas, Stephens, Moicano, Vera, and Font give Aldo real scored value even with the WEC run treated as historical context.
notes: UFC featherweight and bantamweight value is strong, but WEC greatness is historical context only.
Prime/late-prime loss context: McGregor KO, Holloway TKO twice, and Volkanovski decision. Yan/Merab are treated as post…
Aldo’s resume in this ranking is strong but complicated. His WEC run is historical context, while the scored UFC portio…
His championship case is strong, but not fully captured by this model because part of his legendary reign happened befo…

fighters.name: Matt Hughes
fighters.oneLiner: The early welterweight title-control case: real championship volume, physical dominance, and one of the defining reigns before the GSP era.
fighters.titleSummary: Hughes has major early-welterweight championship volume and spent years as the face of the UFC welterweight t…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime was longer and more title-heavy than many compact-resume fighters, even with era-strength context.
fighters.bestArgument: Hughes ranks #12 because his UFC welterweight title volume is still meaningful. He spent years as the division standard, stacked title wins…
fighters.counter: The knock on Hughes is era context. His welterweight run was historically huge, but the division was not as deep or modernized as later wel…
fighters.edge: Hughes wins when the debate becomes total title weight and sustained divisional control rather than pure peak efficiency.
bestWins: GSP, Penn, Royce Gracie, Sherk, Trigg, and Newton give Hughes real championship-era weight at welterweight.
notes: Early welterweight title value is respected, but division depth is not treated like the later GSP era.
Losses to elite names and later-career damage create real drag, especially once GSP separates himself.
Hughes has real championship depth: title-fight volume, sustained divisional control, and a long run as one of the spor…
His best argument is championship weight. Hughes did not just win a belt; he spent years as the face of the welterweigh…

fighters.name: Daniel Cormier
fighters.oneLiner: The compact two-division champion case: heavyweight gold, light heavyweight gold, elite wins, and rivalry ceilings against Jones and Stipe.
fighters.titleSummary: Cormier won UFC gold at light heavyweight and heavyweight, defended both belts, and built a dense title-fight…
fighters.primeSummary: His UFC prime was compact but packed with title fights, elite opponents, and very few low-value appearances.
fighters.bestArgument: Cormier ranks #13 because his UFC resume is compact but elite: two-division champion, strong title-fight wins, and high-level wins at both…
fighters.counter: The counterargument is direct separation. Jones beat him, and Stipe won the trilogy, so Cormier has two major rivalry ceilings in the all-t…
fighters.edge: Cormier wins comparisons when two-division title value and elite opponent quality outweigh rivalry losses to other all-time greats.
bestWins: Stipe, Anthony Johnson twice, Gustafsson, Oezdemir, Derrick Lewis, Dan Henderson, and Anderson Silva give Cormier a strong UFC title-level win list.
notes: Two-division UFC value helps, but both divisions have rivalry ceilings.
Jones and Stipe losses are elite losses, but they meaningfully cap the all-time case.
Cormier’s UFC resume is compact but elite: two-division champion, strong title-fight wins, and major victories over con…
The championship case is strong because he won UFC titles in two divisions and defended both belts, even if the Jones r…

fighters.name: Stipe Miocic
fighters.oneLiner: The strongest UFC heavyweight resume case: title defenses, champion wins, Ngannou value, and trilogy separation over Cormier.
fighters.titleSummary: Stipe’s title case is the UFC heavyweight standard: repeated title wins, multiple defenses, and the Cormier t…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime lasted through several heavyweight waves, with enough durability and adaptability to survive a historically v…
fighters.bestArgument: Stipe ranks #14 because he has the strongest UFC heavyweight resume: heavyweight title wins, major defenses, champion-level opponent qualit…
fighters.counter: The counterargument is division ceiling. Heavyweight is chaotic and thinner than lightweight or welterweight, so Stipe needs championship w…
fighters.edge: Stipe wins comparisons when heavyweight title value, champion wins, and the Cormier trilogy matter more than lighter-division depth.
bestWins: Werdum, Overeem, dos Santos, Ngannou, Cormier twice, Hunt, Arlovski, and Nelson give Stipe the strongest UFC heavyweight win list.
notes: Heavyweight volatility is acknowledged, while Stipe still gets strong credit as the UFC heavyweight standard.
Cormier and Ngannou losses matter, and the late Jones loss adds back-end context.
Stipe’s UFC case is heavyweight-specific but very strong. Heavyweight careers are volatile, and he still built one of t…
The championship case is his separator: heavyweight title wins, defenses, the first Ngannou win, and the Cormier trilog…

fighters.name: Ilia Topuria
fighters.oneLiner: The new-era takeover case: massive featherweight legend wins, elite finishing threat, and one current-table Gaethje loss adding the first r…
fighters.titleSummary: Topuria’s title case is not long yet, but beating Volkanovski and Holloway gives it rare high-end featherweig…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime is still building, which makes the ceiling exciting but keeps the all-time case less proven than the long-rei…
fighters.bestArgument: Topuria ranks #15 because the high end is already enormous. Beating Volkanovski and Holloway gives him direct value against featherweight h…
fighters.counter: The argument against Topuria is that he is still early. The top wins are huge, but he has not had time to build a long title reign, and the…
fighters.edge: Topuria wins comparisons when current peak value and direct wins over featherweight legends outweigh older fighters’ longer volume.
bestWins: Volkanovski, Holloway, Emmett, Mitchell, Ryan Hall, and Jai Herbert give Topuria a young but already serious featherweight resume.
notes: Modern featherweight strength helps, especially because the key wins directly touch featherweight history.
Current-table Gaethje loss is counted as Topuria's first UFC blemish and prime elite-loss context.
Topuria’s UFC case is still young, but the high end is already enormous because the Volkanovski and Holloway wins hit d…
The championship case is smaller than the long-reign champions, but the title wins carry massive name value and era-shi…

fighters.name: Petr Yan
fighters.oneLiner: A modern bantamweight title case with elite skill, strong round control, and unusual DQ context that needs more nuance than a normal loss.
fighters.titleSummary: Yan’s title case is a real bantamweight champion profile, but it is compact: title win, interim title-level v…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime argument is built on round control and technical separation, with major rivalry context against Sterling and…
fighters.bestArgument: Yan ranks here because his UFC-only case has real bantamweight title value, strong elite-round control, and enough quality-win/context cred…
fighters.counter: Yan’s best counterargument is context. The Sterling DQ should not be treated like a normal competitive title loss, the Sterling rematch was…
fighters.edge: Yan wins comparisons when clean skill, round control, title-rematch context, and DQ/loss-context nuance matter more than raw title-defense…
bestWins: Aldo, Sandhagen, Faber, Dodson, and the Merab title-rematch value give Yan a compact but real UFC bantamweight win stack.
notes: Modern bantamweight is treated as a strong division context.
Sterling DQ receives special context; later elite losses count but are not treated like non-elite collapse losses.
Yan’s UFC resume has a compact but serious bantamweight title case. Aldo and Sandhagen anchor the title-race value, whi…
Yan won the UFC bantamweight title, won an interim title-level fight, and later regained title-level value against Mera…

fighters.name: Israel Adesanya
fighters.oneLiner: The modern middleweight title-volume case: elite striking, repeated defenses, a title regain, and one of the deepest active title windows o…
fighters.titleSummary: Adesanya’s title case is built on a strong middleweight reign, repeated defenses, and a title regain after th…
fighters.primeSummary: His elite window was active and title-heavy, with frequent five-round fights against the best middleweights of his era.
fighters.bestArgument: Adesanya ranks #15 because his middleweight championship volume is real: title win, repeated defenses, a title regain, and a deep list of m…
fighters.counter: The counterargument is that the later losses and Pereira rivalry make the case less clean than the longest-reigning champions above him.
fighters.edge: Adesanya wins comparisons when modern middleweight title volume, striking dominance, and repeated elite wins outweigh messy rivalry context.
bestWins: Whittaker twice, Costa, Cannonier, Vettori twice, Gastelum, Pereira, Brunson, and Silva give Adesanya one of the best modern middleweight win lists.
notes: Modern middleweight quality is respected, with the light heavyweight attempt treated as context rather than the core ca…
Pereira, Strickland, and DDP losses matter, with Pereira also adding a redemption win.
Adesanya’s UFC case is built on middleweight title volume and consistency, with the Pereira rivalry adding both a blemi…
His championship case is excellent at middleweight: title win, repeated defenses, a title regain, and multiple wins ove…

fighters.name: Cain Velasquez
fighters.oneLiner: The heavyweight pressure machine: elite pace, wrestling, cardio, and one of the best primes in UFC heavyweight history.
fighters.titleSummary: Cain’s title case is a strong but compact heavyweight reign built around the JDS rivalry and elite pressure d…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime was outstanding and high-paced, but injuries kept it from becoming a long all-time reign.
fighters.bestArgument: Cain ranks here because his prime dominance at heavyweight was exceptional. His pace, wrestling pressure, and cardio made him one of the mo…
fighters.counter: Cain’s argument is peak. If the debate is who looked like the most overwhelming heavyweight at his best, Cain has one of the strongest lane…
fighters.edge: Cain wins comparisons when peak pressure, heavyweight dominance, and the JDS trilogy matter more than title-volume depth.
bestWins: Junior dos Santos twice, Brock Lesnar, Bigfoot Silva twice, Nogueira, and Rothwell give Cain a compact but serious heavyweight resume.
notes: Heavyweight volatility is acknowledged, while Cain gets strong credit for a historically dominant prime.
JDS and Werdum losses matter, but injuries and heavyweight volatility provide context.
Cain’s UFC resume is high-end but compact. The JDS trilogy carries major value, but injuries and limited title volume k…
The championship case is real but not massive: he won the heavyweight belt, regained it, and defended, but did not buil…

fighters.name: Merab Dvalishvili

fighters.titleSummary: Merab’s title case is a major modern bantamweight run, but the rivalry context keeps it from being a simple l…
fighters.primeSummary: His prime is a pressure-control prime: less about finishes, more about making elite opponents fight his pace for five r…
fighters.bestArgument: Merab ranks here because his modern bantamweight run has serious depth. The pace, wrestling volume, and quality wins in a strong division g…
fighters.counter: Merab’s argument is depth and pace. Even with Yan context, the broader modern bantamweight schedule makes his case stronger than a simple t…

bestWins: Yan, Cejudo, Aldo, Moraes, O’Malley, Umar, and ranked bantamweight wins give Merab one of the strongest modern 135-pound ledgers.

Yan rivalry context matters; losses are not treated like a collapse of the whole case.
Merab’s resume is built on modern bantamweight depth. Yan, Cejudo, Aldo, O’Malley, Umar, and other ranked wins give him…
Merab’s championship case is strong but still newer than the long-reign legends, with the Yan rivalry adding direct con…

fighters.name: Charles Oliveira
fighters.oneLiner: The late-blooming lightweight chaos case: elite finishing value, huge quality wins, and a messy record that keeps the score volatile.
fighters.titleSummary: Oliveira’s title case is short but valuable: belt win, defense-level value, and elite lightweight finishes.
fighters.primeSummary: His late lightweight peak was elite and violent, but not long enough to erase the earlier and later losses.
fighters.bestArgument: Oliveira ranks here because his high-end lightweight run is impossible to ignore. Chandler, Poirier, Gaethje, Dariush, Ferguson, and Lee gi…
fighters.counter: Oliveira’s argument is quality and danger. He beat elite lightweights in violent fashion and was never a normal champion to prepare for.
fighters.edge: Oliveira wins comparisons when lightweight quality wins, finishing value, and prime chaos outweigh record cleanliness.
bestWins: Chandler, Poirier, Gaethje, Dariush, Ferguson, and Lee give Oliveira a dangerous lightweight finishing resume.
notes: Lightweight strength is a major positive for his quality-win score.
The record is messy, with early and title-level losses creating real drag.
Oliveira’s resume is high-value and messy. The lightweight peak is elite, but the full UFC record carries too much dama…
His championship case is strong but short: belt win, one defense, and title-level wins that felt bigger because of the…

fighters.name: B.J. Penn
fighters.oneLiner: The brilliant-but-messy skill case: lightweight gold, the Hughes welterweight upset, elite talent, and a late record collapse that drags th…
fighters.titleSummary: Penn won UFC titles at lightweight and welterweight, with the Hughes upset and lightweight defenses carrying…
fighters.primeSummary: His best UFC years were brilliant but not long enough to erase the late-career collapse.
fighters.bestArgument: Penn ranks here because the high-end UFC case is still real: lightweight champion, welterweight champion, the Hughes upset, and a peak skil…
fighters.counter: Penn’s argument is peak skill and two-division greatness. The argument against him is the record collapse and too many damaging losses afte…
fighters.edge: Penn wins comparisons when peak skill, lightweight title value, and the Hughes upset outweigh a messy career arc.
bestWins: Hughes twice, Sherk, Florian, Sanchez, Stevenson, Pulver, and Uno give Penn a real UFC-only win list, even without using non-UFC achievements as scoring value.
notes: Lightweight title value and the Hughes welterweight upset both matter; non-UFC achievements are context only.
Late-career losses create major drag and keep the case below cleaner champions.
Penn’s UFC case has huge highs and ugly lows. The Hughes win and lightweight title run are real all-time material, but…
The championship case is strong because Penn won UFC titles at lightweight and welterweight, then defended lightweight…

fighters.name: Junior dos Santos
fighters.oneLiner: A UFC heavyweight champion with a historic nine-fight rise, elite Cain/Werdum/Stipe wins, and a ceiling capped by the Cain rematches.
fighters.titleSummary: UFC heavyweight champion with one successful title defense and 1.85 adjusted title wins.
fighters.primeSummary: Historic heavyweight rise with elite boxing power, then Cain rivalry cap.
fighters.bestArgument: The best argument is that very few heavyweights in this range have three wins as strong as Cain, Werdum, and Stipe.
fighters.counter: The counterargument is championship volume: only one defense, and Cain took the belt back decisively.
fighters.edge: Dos Santos wins comparisons when heavyweight opponent quality and KO peak matter more than long title-control volume.
bestWins: Cain Velasquez, Fabricio Werdum, Stipe Miocic, Frank Mir, Shane Carwin, Mark Hunt, Derrick Lewis.
notes: Heavyweight volatility matters, but the Cain/Werdum/Stipe/Carwin/Mir/Hunt top end gives the resume real strength.
Cain II, Cain III, and Overeem are the counted damage. The later heavyweight losing streak is post-prime decline and co…
The resume top end is excellent for heavyweight, but the title reign is short and the Cain rivalry caps the ceiling.
One UFC heavyweight title win and one defense give real championship value, but not enough volume to match long-reign c…

fighters.name: Tito Ortiz
fighters.oneLiner: A five-defense early UFC light heavyweight king with real title volume, huge star aura, and a ceiling capped by era strength plus Randy/Chu…
fighters.primeRecord: 10-4 in early UFC LHW title/elite window
fighters.titleSummary: UFC light heavyweight champion with five successful title defenses and 5.20 adjusted title wins.
fighters.primeSummary: Early UFC LHW control reign with major star aura, then clear ceiling against Randy and Chuck.
fighters.bestArgument: The best argument is simple: five UFC title defenses is championship volume most fighters in this rank zone cannot match.
fighters.counter: The counterargument is era depth: the defense count is big, but the opponent slate and Chuck/Randy losses cap the all-time ceiling.

Five successful title defenses give Tito real championship meat, even after discounting the early-era defense slate.

fighters.name: Deiveson Figueiredo
fighters.oneLiner: A violent two-time UFC flyweight champion with real Moreno-rivalry title value, elite Pantoja/Benavidez wins, and useful bantamweight depth.
fighters.titleSummary: Two-time UFC flyweight champion with three official title-fight wins plus draw-retainment context.
fighters.primeSummary: Violent flyweight title prime with strong finishing threat and messy Moreno-rivalry ceiling.

fighters.name: Khamzat Chimaev

fighters.primeSummary: Explosive elite prime with a huge control profile, but not enough title-defense volume yet.
fighters.bestArgument: The best argument is that few fighters in this rank zone have a scarier best version or a cleaner high-end dominance profile.
fighters.counter: The counterargument is short resume: not enough title fights, not enough elite years, and not enough volume yet.

fighters.name: Michael Bisping

fighters.counter: The counter is that he was never a dominant champion and did not clear the strongest post-title contender line.

fighters.titleSummary: One-time UFC women’s bantamweight champion with one official and adjusted title-fight win.
fighters.primeSummary: 5-1 prime from Carmouche through Nunes, capped by the Nunes title loss.
fighters.bestArgument: The best argument is that beating Holm for the belt is a legitimate top-end UFC championship moment.
fighters.counter: The counterargument is thin UFC-only depth: one title-fight win, no defenses, a 7-7 UFC record, and limited elite wins outside Holm.
fighters.edge: Tate wins comparisons when a real undisputed title win and signature championship comeback matter more than clean record volume.
bestWins: Holly Holm, Sara McMann, Liz Carmouche, Jessica Eye, Marion Reneau, Julia Avila.
notes: Women’s bantamweight was historically important during Tate’s UFC title climb, but the division depth is not treated as…

Her UFC resume is not deep, but the Holm title win gives her a real champion case. The model rewards that moment withou…
One UFC bantamweight title win, no defenses, and two UFC title-fight losses.

fighters.name: Holly Holm
fighters.oneLiner: A former UFC bantamweight champion with one immortal title upset, but no defenses and too many failed elite/title spots to climb higher.
fighters.titleSummary: One-time UFC bantamweight champion.
fighters.primeSummary: Title/elite window from Ronda through Aldana.
fighters.bestArgument: She authored one of the biggest UFC wins ever.
fighters.counter: The counter is everything after Ronda: no defenses, immediate Miesha loss, and repeated title-fight failures.
fighters.edge: Holm wins comparisons when signature moment and historical shock value matter.
bestWins: Ronda Rousey, Irene Aldana, Raquel Pennington, Megan Anderson.
notes: Women’s bantamweight title era plus featherweight title attempts, with reduced credit/penalty for cross-division contex…
Miesha, Valentina, Nunes, Vieira, and title failures are the scoring damage; Kayla is post-prime.
The résumé is top-heavy: Ronda is huge, but the supporting championship case is thin.
One UFC bantamweight title win, zero defenses.

fighters.name: Mackenzie Dern
fighters.oneLiner: A current UFC strawweight champion with elite submission danger and real title value, but a volatile contender ledger keeps the UFC-only sc…
fighters.titleSummary: One-time UFC strawweight champion with one official title-fight win and 0.90 adjusted title wins.
fighters.primeSummary: 9-4 prime from Cifers through current champ phase.
fighters.bestArgument: The best argument is that Dern is a current UFC champion with one of the strongest submission threats in women’s UFC history.
fighters.counter: The counterargument is volatility: five UFC losses, no defenses, and no reigning-champion win yet.
fighters.edge: Dern wins comparisons when current-title value, submission threat, and long strawweight activity matter more than clean championship contro…
bestWins: Virna Jandiroba twice, Amanda Ribas, Tecia Torres, Angela Hill, Loopy Godinez, Nina Nunes.
notes: Women’s strawweight is treated as a strong women’s UFC division across Dern’s contender/title window.
Amanda Ribas I is pre-prime. Marina Rodriguez, Yan Xiaonan, Jessica Andrade, and Amanda Lemos are counted prime losses;…
The UFC resume has volume and a belt, but the elite-win ceiling is still building. The model rewards the title without…
One UFC strawweight title win, no defenses yet, with adjusted title value discounted for the vacant-title path.

specialMatchups.coreDebate: Usman has the better championship reign and stronger peak control. Holloway has the deeper overall case because of elite longevity and quality-win volume.
notes: Avoid raw category point totals.

specialMatchups.coreDebate: GSP is the cleaner case. Jones is the bigger championship-volume and top-end case.
notes: If clean record/controversy is weighted heavily, GSP has the easier counterargument.

specialMatchups.coreDebate: Khabib has the cleaner peak. Islam has the fuller current championship and modern-depth case.
notes: Use Khabib undefeated run and Islam title-fight volume as the swing points.
